{{< pagebreak >}}

# A compassionate restart {#restart}

At some point the practice will slip — a busy stretch, a trip, or simply life. That
is normal, and it happens to everyone. In a real-world study, missing a single day
did not stop a habit from forming;^11^ a habit is not a fragile streak that
shatters the moment you miss. The awareness you built is still there. You simply
step back onto the path.

```{=typst}
#block(width: 100%, fill: c-aside, stroke: (left: 3pt + c-gold), radius: (right: 4pt), inset: (x: 16pt, y: 13pt), above: 12pt, below: 12pt)[
  #text(font: ("Manrope",), size: 10pt, weight: 700, tracking: 2pt, fill: c-gold)[WHAT THE RESEARCH SUGGESTS]
  #v(5pt)
  #text(font: ("Manrope",), size: 11.5pt, fill: c-body)[
    Responding to a slip with self-kindness rather than harsh criticism is linked
    with more motivation to try again#super[12] and with lower stress when we fall
    short of what we intended.#super[17]
  ]
]
```

Fill this in now, while things are calm, so it is ready for the day you need it.

```{=typst}
#wcg-field("The smallest first step I can take", lines: 2)
#wcg-field("A kind sentence to say to myself", lines: 3)
#wcg-field("When I will begin again", lines: 1)
```

> **A gentle reminder.**
>
> One missed week is just one week. Being kind to yourself here is practical, not
> soft — it is what makes beginning again easy.
